pick.rationale: This is an unusually competitive 7/10 matchup where Santa Clara actually boasts superior offensive production (ranked 15th-23rd nationally vs Kentucky's 39th) and a significantly better record (26-8 vs 21-13). However, Kentucky's SEC pedigree combined with their substantially better defensive efficiency (32nd nationally at 99.1 adjD vs Santa Clara's 76th at 104.3 adjD) gives them the edge in a single-elimination format where defense often prevails. While the efficiency margins are nearly identical (+21.47 vs +19.42), Kentucky's tournament experience as a Power 5 program and ability to slow down opponents makes them the slight favorite despite this being one of the closest first-round matchups on paper.

reasoningStep.title: Kentucky vs Santa Clara Matchup Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Competitive 7/10 seed matchup with surprisingly close advanced metrics favoring neither team decisively
reasoningStep.evidence: Kentucky: Torvik 32/KenPom 28, 21-13 SEC record, AdjO 120.5 (39th), AdjD 99.1 (32nd), AdjEM +21.47 | Santa Clara: Torvik 28/KenPom 35, 26-8 WCC record, AdjO 123.7-125.0 (15th-23rd), AdjD 103.6-104.3 (76th), AdjEM +19.42. Kentucky's defensive advantage and SEC pedigree outweigh Santa Clara's offensive superiority and better record in tournament play.

You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.

Kentucky (7-seed):
- Torvik rank: 32, KenPom rank: 28

- SEC conference

Santa Clara (10-seed):

- WCC conference
- AdjO: ~123.7-125.0 (15th-23rd nationally) - actually BETTER than Kentucky's offense!
- AdjD: ~103.6-104.3 (76th nationally) - worse defense than Kentucky
- AdjEM: +19.42

This is fascinating - Santa Clara actually has better offensive metrics and a better record (26-8 vs 21-13), but Kentucky has better defensive metrics and slightly better overall efficiency margin. The rankings are surprisingly close - in fact, Santa Clara ranks higher in Torvik (28 vs 32).

This is NOT the typical 7/10 seed matchup where the 7-seed dominates. In fact, this looks like one of the closest matchups in the Round of 64 based on advanced metrics alone.

- Kentucky (7-seed): Torvik 32, KenPom 28, 21-13 record, SEC conference

This is actually a very unusual matchup where the 10-seed has BETTOR offensive metrics and a significantly better record (26-8 vs 21-13). The efficiency margins are also very close (+21.47 for Kentucky vs +19.42 for Santa Clara).

Historically, 7-seeds win about 75-80% of matchups against 10-seeds. However, this doesn't look like a typical matchup - Santa Clara's offense is ranked 15th-23rd nationally while Kentucky's is only 39th. Santa Clara also has a much better winning percentage (.765 vs .617).

The key question is whether Kentucky's SEC pedigree and slightly better defense can overcome Santa Clara's superior offense and record. In tournament play, conference strength often matters more than raw metrics, and Kentucky's SEC background should give them an edge in terms of tournament experience and competition level.

However, this is genuinely competitive. I'll lean toward Kentucky but with lower confidence than usual for a 7-seed. The rationale should acknowledge how close this matchup is compared to typical 7/10 games.

Let me make my pick: Kentucky wins, but with moderate confidence given how close the metrics are.

Historically, 7-seeds win about 75-80% of matchups against 10-seeds. However, this matchup defies typical patterns - the 10-seed has superior offense and record.

Key factors favoring Kentucky:
- SEC pedigree (higher competition level)

- Tournament experience as a Power 5 program

Key factors favoring Santa Clara:

I'll pick Kentucky with moderate confidence (around 58-62%) because while the metrics are surprisingly close, the SEC pedigree and tournament experience should give them the edge in a first-round game. This is not a dominant favorite situation like most 7-seeds face.

Despite Santa Clara's superior offense and record, I'm picking Kentucky because:
